Because whether we like it or not, life moves. It breathes. It was never designed to stay still. Our entire human history is proof that stagnation isn't our nature. Growth is. We wake up. We think the same thoughts. We do pretty much the same things. We make the same choices and call it stability. But what if it's not? What if it's just habit? And from a very human point of view, I think that that of support would give us the courage to meet change with confidence. The makeup artist, the set designer, the costume team, and a dozen other people you'll never ever see in the credits. All holding the moment with you. When I arrived in France, I was a teenager in high school. It was not long after 9/11 attacks 2001. The world felt tense. Islamophobia was everywhere. in the news, in the streets, in the air. As I was struggling to find my place in this society, I was trying to be friends with this girl at school. So, one day as I was walking back to my class, she was walking right in front of me. So, I quickly caught up with her in my excitement to strike a conversation. She had never met a person of a Pakistani origin before me. She had this image of the region and much of the world and much of the Muslim world as something distant, something frightening, something to be feared. And all she thought she knew came from what she had seen on a TV screen after an attack. So I did what felt natural. I argued. I gave her facts. I told her people of the region are also victims of terrorism. I told her we are good people. I was one standing right in front of her. She said she didn't believe it. So after a few frustrated attempts at trying to convince her to change her image of my origins, I asked her directly, "Do you think you can ever change your opinion?" She said, "No." She said the damage was done, that it was too late now and it cannot be reversed. The conversation ended in silence and we both parted our ways back to our classes. As a teenager, I felt sad. I felt judged and completely blocked out. She was not protecting an image, a perception of a region, a country or a religion. She was protecting a feeling of safety in a world that had just been made frightening. And no fact I gave her could touch that feeling because we were answering the wrong question entirely. What has happened to me then was not my first encounter. Over the years, I was asked if I was a Taliban. I was asked why am I not wearing a burka and how come my parents are allowing me to have an education? All because of where I came from. Every time I was shocked at how quickly people judged me based on where I was born. Every time I doubled down on my arguments and they on theirs. Now what has happened to me has happened to every single one of us in one way or the other. Look at our campuses. Look at our parliaments. Look at our dinner tables and our common sections. People are not talking with each other. They're talking at each other. And it is strange because we live in the most connected times in human history. We have more information than ever, more ways to connect with the other than ever. Yet, we're more divided than ever. I want you to imagine something. Imagine you're sitting across from someone who represents everything you oppose. Your pulse quickens, your chest tightens, and first you avoid the conversation altogether. But then when you do talk, you come armed with opinions, with facts, with arguments and stats, ready to dismantle their worldview, convinced that if you just say it better, louder, clearer, they will finally understand. But here's what the research tells us. It does not work. Why? Because we're taught to argue. We're taught to debate, but we are not taught to listen and to see. The best way to create a space for a better understanding is not through a better argument. It is through what I like to call recognition. Recognition is the radical act of acknowledging another person's dignity and the legitimacy of their perspective, no matter where it comes from, without requiring your agreement. We think we are defending ideas, but we're actually defending our identities. Many of us feel that changing our minds is a form of social suicide and a betrayal to the group that gives us our sense of belonging. Recognition bypasses that threat. It can it separates the person from the opinion, the relationship from the debate. So in order to understand why this matters, we have to see what happens inside of us when someone challenges what we believe in. Because believe me, we are not bad at arguing. We are bad at understanding what arguing does to people. Psychologist Jonathan height did a study to understand how people form opinions. He found that we do not reason our ways to our beliefs. We feel our way there first and then we use logic to defend what we already believe. When our world view is challenged, our body releases cortisol, a stress hormone that hijacks our rational thinking and activates fight or flight. In that state, logic disappears. We stop listening and we focus entirely on pushing back. Now, what actually works? Then we have empirical evidence that recognition succeeds where facts fail. In 2016, researchers from Stanford and UC Berkeley conducted a door-to-door realworld experiment where they sent two groups to compare two different ways of discussing a deeply divisive political issue of transgender rights. One group went to people's homes with facts, arguments, and statistics. The other group did something very different. They went and had a 10-minute conversation asking people about their lives. They listened and reflected back. The first group with facts, almost no lasting change was seen with the people they communicated. The second group, the listening group, significant attitude shifts were seen even nine months later. nine months from a 10-minute conversation, not because they had a better information, but because they felt seen. The most powerful example I know of this is of a man named Daryl Davis. Daryl Davis is a black jazz musician from the USA. In 1983, he did something most people thought impossible. He started meeting the members of Ku Klux Clan, not to debate them, not to argue them. He had one question for them and he meant it. How can you hate me if you don't even know me? How can you hate me if you don't even know me? And then he listened. He did not agree with them. He did not pretend their worldview was okay. but he saw them as human beings with fears and a story. Over the next 30 years of his engagement, more than 200 clan members left the organization. And in the words of Daryl Davis himself, he did not convert anyone. They converted themselves. He just created the conditions where that was possible. That's the power of recognition. It removes threat and when there is no threat, the door opens from the inside. But this is not just true between two people. We have seen this happening at the scale of a country when understood. In 1995, Nelson Mandela became the first black president of a South Africa that had every reason to fall apart. To most black South Africans, the Springbog rugby jersey was a symbol of apartheid of a regime that had put him in prison for 27 years. The logical move was to remove that symbol. Mandela did the exact opposite. He appeared in front of a worldwide audience wearing the famous green and gold jersey at the 1995 Rugby World Cup final. He recognized what this meant for the white Africaners, their identity and their fear of being erased in a new South Africa. People, the crowd who grew up calling him a terrorist began chanting his name. Mandela did not win them with an argument. No, he warned them because he showed them that he understood what they were afraid of losing. Recognition at the scale of a country open the broader doors for reconciliation. This matters more today than it ever has. diplomats negotiating among nations, leaders trying to hold divided societies together. And in the world of artificial intelligence, we have unlimited arguments, fast, cheap, tailored perfectly. In this world, arguments are worth nothing because we all have them. What is rare, truly rare, is that somebody actually sees you, that somebody actually trusts you. So, how do we do this in our daily lives? How do you and I do this in our daily lives? I'm going to share with you a tool that I call the three R method. Three steps, very simple, anyone can do it. First recognize the stake. Ask in your conversation. What is it that matters to you the most? Number two, reflect it back. Show that you have truly understood what they care about. And third, reframe the disagreement. mention what's happening and what both parties are protecting and from there move towards a solution. This is recognition without agreement. You can keep your conviction with an open mind. You just have to stop treating the other person as an enemy to defeat. That girl in my high school could not hear me. Not because I was wrong, but because being right was not enough. I was protecting an image of a region. I was protecting myself. She was protecting a fear. She was protecting herself. And we both talked past each other. I cannot go back. But you right now have a choice. In every conversation, that's important. Next time you're in a disagreement that matters, stop. Put down your facts and arguments. Ask a question. Listen. Listen until they feel heard. Listen until you understand. People do not change because you are right. People change because they felt safe enough to reconsider. And sometimes the most powerful thing that you can say is simply I hear you. Thank you.
